shortDescription.text: TOOL_FILE transfer method bypasses file type validation

fullDescription.text: validation.py unconditionally allows TOOL_FILES, bypassing the normal file type restriction logic. An attacker who can influence the transfer method to TOOL_FILE can upload arbitrary file types (e.g., .exe, .html, .svg) that would otherwise be rejected. This policy circumvention could enable stored XSS via SVG uploads, malware distribution, or server-side code execution depending on downstream handling.

shortDescription.text: Potential SSRF bypass in remote file metadata fetching via HEAD request

fullDescription.text: The remote.py module fetches remote file metadata via HEAD requests with SSRF protections. However, SSRF mitigations based on URL parsing (urllib.parse) and regex validation (re.search) are frequently bypassable through DNS rebinding, URL parser inconsistencies (e.g., backslash handling, unicode normalization, authority confusion), IPv6 bracket encoding, or redirect-based attacks if the HTTP client follows redirects. The HEAD request itself may also be escalated to a full GET by some servers or proxies.

shortDescription.text: Filename extracted from Content-Disposition header may allow path traversal

fullDescription.text: The remote.py module parses Content-Disposition headers for filename extraction using RFC5987 encoding. A malicious server can supply a crafted Content-Disposition header with directory traversal sequences (e.g., filename*=UTF-8''..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd or filename="../../etc/cron.d/exploit"). If the extracted filename is used in filesystem operations without sanitization (stripping path separators and normalizing), it could lead to arbitrary file write or overwrite outside the intended directory.

shortDescription.text: File factory applies inconsistent validation across transfer methods

fullDescription.text: builders.py constructs File objects from multiple transfer methods (LOCAL_FILE, REMOTE_URL, TOOL_FILE, DATASOURCE_FILE) but validation is not uniformly applied. TOOL_FILE explicitly bypasses validation in validation.py. This creates a confused deputy scenario where internal components trusting the TOOL_FILE designation may process attacker-controlled content without appropriate sanitization, enabling file type confusion attacks or content-type mismatches that downstream consumers may not expect.

message.text: TOOL_FILE transfer method unconditionally bypasses file type validation in validation.py. An attacker who can control the transfer_method parameter (e.g., via API parameter tampering or through a compromised plugin/tool) can upload arbitrary file types that would normally be rejected, potentially enabling stored XSS (via SVG), malware distribution, or server-side exploitation.

Remote file metadata fetching via HEAD request uses urllib.parse and re.search for SSRF protection. These mitigations are known to be bypassable through DNS rebinding, URL parser differential exploits (backslash normalization, unicode confusables, authority segment confusion), IPv6 bracket notation, or redirect chains. Verify that: (1) DNS resolution is validated at connection time (not just URL parse time), (2) redirects are blocked or re-validated, (3) the HTTP client does not silently upgrade HEAD to GET.

Filename extraction from Content-Disposition header (RFC5987) in remote.py may be vulnerable to path traversal. A malicious remote server can supply crafted filenames containing '../' sequences or absolute paths. If the extracted filename is passed to file I/O operations without basename extraction and path normalization, an attacker can write files outside the intended upload directory.

File factory in builders.py applies different validation logic per transfer method. TOOL_FILE bypasses all file type checks, while LOCAL_FILE and REMOTE_URL are validated. This inconsistency creates a privilege escalation path: if an attacker can influence the transfer_method to TOOL_FILE (e.g., through API manipulation or a malicious tool/plugin), they bypass the entire validation layer. All transfer methods should pass through uniform validation with explicit allowlisting per method rather than blanket bypass.
